steps.text: Determine whether the licensee’s fuel handling procedures are adequate to perform intended functions.
Determine whether fuel is moved and inspected consistent with the requirements of the TS and the licensee’s procedures.
Determine whether fuel handling activities are conducted in accordance with 10 CFR Part 20 and the licensee’s procedures and programs for radiation protection.
Determine whether the licensee satisfied security plan requirements for fuel movement activities.
Determine whether significant fuel movement or inspection problems are identified and resolved in accordance with the licensee’s procedural controls.
Determine whether the licensee was within TS limits and met procedural requirements before resuming normal operation after fuel movement.
Be aware of the facility’s plans and schedules for refueling or other major fuel movement.

Review the safety analysis report (SAR) or as-built facility descriptions, the TS, the licensee’s procedure(s), and the Security Plan for fuel design information, fuel movement methods, and requirements.
During the onsite inspection, be alert for significant differences in the SAR, or the TS descriptions of fuel and equipment as compared to the actual fuel and equipment.
Ensure design change review and approval is performed in accordance with 10 CFR 50.59 and the licensee’s procedures.
Observe one fuel movement activity.
Verify the final fuel location.
Review respective records.
Conduct discussions with personnel involved in the most recent fuel movement activity.
Verify if the final location of the fuel is consistent with that specified in the licensee's records.
Conduct dry runs for operations of potential safety concern or of an unusual nature, including discussion of actions needed to avert or mitigate accidents.
Transfer fuel into a core configuration, or to racks, containers, or storage pits designed to prevent accidental criticality.
Remove only one bundle at a time to be inspected underwater.
Replace the inspected bundle before proceeding.
Perform 1/M calculations independently by more than one person.
Inspect fuel and control rods in accordance with the TS and procedures.
Periodically inspect the fuel and visually determine its integrity.
Check the control rods to determine their capability to continue to function.
Investigate and resolve control rod failures to ensure they perform their intended shutdown function.
Inspect the removed neutron source for leakage paths such as splitting or pitting.
Monitor or sample for airborne radioactive particulate matter.
Monitor exits from controlled areas.
Perform surveys for contamination, including licensee personnel, tools, and equipment upon exit of the work area.
Monitor radiation areas periodically to determine whether actual radiation levels are consistent.

Verify personnel monitoring devices (such as film badges, TLDs, direct reading dosimeters, and extremity monitors) are worn correctly and are appropriate for expected radiation fields and the type of work.
Observe security controls during one fuel movement activity.
Review records and conduct discussions with responsible personnel for the most recent fuel movement activity.
Observe the resolution of one fuel movement problem, or if direct observation is not possible, review the licensee's logs and records and discuss with responsible personnel.
Verify that the licensee resolved identified problems to meet TS requirements.
Observe one reactor startup after fuel movement, or if direct observation is not possible, review respective licensee logs and records and discuss with responsible personnel.

preconditions.text: Significant differences in the SAR, or the TS descriptions of fuel and equipment as compared to the actual fuel and equipment are identified.
Direct observation of a fuel movement activity is not possible at the time of inspection.
Participants must be trained and understand their tasks.
Fuel handling tools must be consistent with TS requirements and licensee procedures.
Calculate the minimum number of fuel bundles for a critical mass with optimum moderation, or ensure bundles are separated by designed fuel racks.
When the licensee is changing the core configuration and 1/M calculations are required.
If an installed neutron source is removed during refueling.
If direct observation of security controls is not possible at the time of inspection
If the detailed records of the tests and checks were covered under the Inspection Procedure on 'Class I Research and Test Reactor Surveillance', this observation/review need not be repeated.

constraints.text: Under no circumstances is the licensee to adjust schedules for these activities to fit the inspection schedule.
The use of approved procedures are required in accordance with TS and security plan requirements.
Ensure that the fuel is not damaged, that accidental criticality does not occur, and that radiation and contamination hazards are minimized.
Failure of a fuel element can be dramatic (e.g., blisters, cracks, high air particulate alarm, evacuation, and substantial water radioactivity).
On a highly under-moderated core, water replacing a removed fuel rod adds more reactivity than the fuel removal takes away.
Four fuel bundles in a square matrix pose a criticality hazard, whereas four in a line do not.
Fuel movement can cause significant radiological hazards. A single fuel element can easily emit radiation at levels above 100 rem per hour at one foot.
Maintain occupational doses as low as reasonably achievable (ALARA).
If area radiation levels are so high that a difference of about 0.2 mr/hr cannot be detected, the detector area may need shielding or the controlled area should be extended to a low radiation area.
Grab sampling must be performed in accordance with approved licensee procedures.
Surveys for contamination must be performed in accordance with licensee procedures and specified frequencies.
Personnel dosimetry is required by 10 CFR 20.1502 and is to be used as specified by the licensee’s procedures.
Licensee personnel are normally instructed to check direct reading dosimeters frequently.
New fuel is required to be kept in the security area and locked in a fuel vault or in some way secured.
Changes to core configuration may require calibration of control rods, and determination of excess reactivity and shutdown margin.
